screen.name: screen1
screen.instruct:
The first step is to fill three vials with 1-propanol, which will serve as our standard.
Use the pipette to place a specific amount of 1-propanol into all three glass vials.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control
1-propanol

screen.name: screen2
screen.instruct:
As a negative control, we’ll use pure 1-Propanol. As a positive control, we’ll use a specific concentration of ethanol, which is the type of alcohol found in alcoholic beverages.
Add a specific amount of ethanol to the vial labeled “positive control”.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control
Ethanol

screen.name: screen3
screen.instruct: Good. Now, use the pipette to add a specific amount of blood to the glass vial labeled “blood”.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control

screen.name: screen4
screen.instruct: Place the vials in the gas chromatograph.
screen.label: blood
Positive control
Negative control

screen.name: screen6
screen.instruct: The results are in. Click on the monitor to analyze the data.

screen.name: screen7
screen.instruct: Comparing the number of peaks in the blood sample to the number of peaks in the controls, what can you conclude?
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: blood sample
Gas chromatogram #2: positive control
Gas chromatogram #3: negative control
Response
Time
screen.option: There's ethanol in the blood sample
There's no ethanol in the blood sample
screen.response:
Look closer at the sample. Does it look like the positive or the negative control?
Hint: we’re only looking at the presence of the peak, not the height. Try again.
Correct. The sample looks like the positive control, which means there’s ethanol present.

screen.name: screen8
screen.instruct:
Can you identify which of the two peaks in the blood sample is the ethanol peak?
Click on the ethanol peak in the blood sample.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: blood sample
Gas chromatogram #2: positive control
Gas chromatogram #3: negative control
Response
Time
screen.response:
Not quite.
Tip: compare the negative control with the positive control, which contained ethanol. Try again.
Correct. This peak is at the same position as the ethanol peak in the positive control.

screen.name: screen9
screen.instruct:
The next step is to determine the victim’s blood alcohol concentration (BAC).
Nowadays, chromatography systems do this calculation automatically, but you need to know the steps involved.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: blood sample
Gas chromatogram #2: positive control
Gas chromatogram #3: negative control
Response
Time

screen.name: screen10
screen.instruct: We’ll start by looking at the height and width of the ethanol peak.
screen.caption: Gas chromatogram #1: blood sample
Gas chromatogram #2: positive control
Gas chromatogram #3: negative control
Response
Time

screen.name: screen11
screen.instruct: Using the units on the x-axis, what is the width (or base) of the ethanol peak?
screen.caption: base (b)
screen.wrongPopup: That's not what I see. Try again.

screen.name: screen12
screen.instruct: Using the units on the y-axis, what is the approximate height of the peak?
screen.caption: b
height (h)
Time
b =
screen.wrongPopup: That's not quite right. Try again.

screen.name: screen13
screen.instruct: Since the peak is similar to an equilateral triangle, we can use the formula for the area of an equilateral triangle to calculate the area of the ethanol peak. Drag the numbers to the formula below.
screen.caption: b
h
Time
b =
h =

screen.name: screen14
screen.instruct: The graph below is called a calibration curve. It shows the peak areas of different samples with a known ethanol concentration. We can use this calibration curve to estimate the ethanol concentration in our victim’s blood.
screen.caption: Peak area
% Vol. Ethanol

screen.name: screen15
screen.instruct: We already know the peak area of our ethanol sample. Click the correct number on the y-axis of the graph.
screen.caption: Peak area
% Vol. Ethanol

screen.name: screen16
screen.instruct: Good. Now, drag a straight line from this value on the y-axis to the calibration curve.
screen.caption: Peak area
% Vol. Ethanol

screen.name: screen17
screen.instruct: Next, draw a straight line from the value on the calibration curve to the x-axis.
screen.caption: Peak area
% Vol. Ethanol

screen.name: screen18
screen.instruct: What is the victim’s blood alcohol concentration (BAC)?
screen.caption: Peak area
% Vol. Ethanol
screen.wrongPopup: That's not what I see. Try again.
